title: Dispatching and paunching a rabbit
subtitle: Cervical dislocation followed by immediate paunching to produce a clean carcass for the table.
excerpt: A rabbit dispatched and paunched within ten minutes produces a better carcass than one left to stiffen before processing. The most common method for small numbers on the smallholding is cervical dislocation, followed by hanging and removing the digestive organs before the body heat dissipates. This tutorial covers both steps.
sourceNotes:
DEFRA, Welfare of Animals at the Time of Killing (England) Regulations 2015 (WATOK). https://www.gov.uk
Farmer and Stockbreeder, small-stock slaughter guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: A method of dispatch that severs the spinal cord by stretching and dislocating the neck at the first cervical vertebra. When performed correctly it causes immediate loss of consciousness and is an approved method for rabbits under Schedule 1 of WATOK 2015.

The removal of the digestive tract (stomach, intestines, and caecum) from a dispatched rabbit. Done immediately after dispatch to prevent gut bacteria contaminating the carcass. The diaphragm, heart, liver, and kidneys are typically left in the body cavity unless the carcass is to be jointed.

content.text: Under Schedule 1 of the Welfare of Animals at the Time of Killing (England) Regulations 2015 (WATOK), owners may dispatch their own rabbits by

without a Certificate of Competence, provided the animal is kept for personal consumption and the method is applied correctly. Do not attempt this method on an animal belonging to another person without appropriate authorisation.

Hold the rabbit by its hind legs in one hand. Place the thumb of the other hand at the base of the skull, fingers under the chin. In a single firm movement, extend the neck sharply while applying upward pressure with the thumb. You will feel the dislocation. The rabbit should go immediately limp and show no purposeful movement. Reflex muscular twitching after dispatch is normal and does not indicate consciousness. If you are uncertain about the method, observe a confident practitioner before attempting it alone.

attrs.title: Using a killing cone
attrs.body: A killing cone restrains the rabbit and positions it head-down, which reduces struggling and keeps the dispatcher's hands free. Slide the rabbit head-first into the cone so only the head emerges. Apply cervical dislocation from below. The cone is not mandatory but makes the task cleaner and calmer, particularly for a beginner.

content.text: Hang the rabbit by one hind leg from a hook. Make a short cut through the skin on the lower belly, avoiding the gut wall. Extend the cut toward the breastbone with scissors or the blade tip. Hold the gut clear of the body wall and cut the oesophagus close to the throat and the rectum close to the pelvis. Remove the entire digestive tract in one piece.
must be complete within 30 minutes of dispatch to prevent gut bacteria tainting the carcass.

Wipe the cavity with a clean cloth; do not wash with water as this speeds deterioration. Chill the carcass to below 4 degrees C within two hours. A rabbit carcass can be skinned before or after chilling: skinning immediately after dispatch is easier when the carcass is still warm. The skin slips off cleanly from the hind legs downward if the rabbit is hung and the skin cut at the hocks and pulled.
